meta.title: ATC boundary splitting — plain English
meta.description: What the spike measured, what the numbers mean, and spot-checks on real transcripts.

props.title: Splitting compound ATC text without punctuation
props.subtitle: A dummy-friendly read: what we built, what we measured, and where ML beats regex (and vice versa).
props.meta: Spot-check on 177 flight ASR lines + 22 fixture Whisper lines · April 2026

props.content: **Problem:** Whisper gives you ONE shouting, ALL-CAPS blob with no commas. **Goal:** chop it into separate instructions so each piece can get its own intent + slots. **This page:** we compare a **small neural net** (boundary tagger) to a **regex verb trick** — not because either is “truth,” but because we need a repeatable way to stress-test both on real transcripts.

Picture a controller cramming three thoughts into one transmission. The **boundary** is the spot where thought B starts.

• **Regex splitter:** hunts for **action verbs** (taxi, turn, contact…) because those often start a new instruction. Fast, dumb, and **slashes too eagerly** when the verb is really part of one phrase (“ready **for** taxi”).

• **ML splitter:** learned from **synthetic ATC** where we glued fake transmissions together. It spots “new thought” patterns in embedding space. It can **merge** things regex rips apart, but it can also **over-chop** long weather/ATIS paragraphs because those look like several sentences.

**Neither is a judge of legal phraseology** — segment *count* is just a cheap first gauge. Readbacks and safety care about *correct* boundaries, which still need gold labels or human review.

props.title: Cheat sheet
props.content: **More segments ≠ smarter.** If regex cuts 4 times on a one-thought line, that’s bad. If ML leaves squawk+taxi glued, that’s also bad. The JSONL report stores both side-by-side so humans (or agents) can grade each row.

props.label: TOOLS
props.title: Regex vs ML — different failure modes
🔧 Verb-boundary regex
props.content:
**Pros:** deterministic, no GPU, easy to audit.
**Cons:** leans on English verbs; unpunctuated ASR breaks assumptions; tends to split before TAXI / DIRECT even inside one clearance.

props.title: 🧠 DeBERTa-base boundary tagger
props.content:
**Pros:** learns collocations; often keeps “ready for taxi” in one chunk.
**Cons:** trained mostly on synthetic glue data; may slice ATIS/weather into too many bites for a single briefing.

rows.meaning: Same number of chunks from both methods.

takeaway.text: Still read both lists — chunks can differ inside.

rows.meaning: Regex chopped extra times (usually at a verb).

takeaway.text: Often regex is over-splitting one clearance.
Often long weather / Flight Service lines.

props.label: SPOT CHECK
props.title: Human eyeballs on real disagreement rows
props.subtitle: Sampled from boundary_transcript_report_full.jsonl — not statistical proof, just intuition.

props.title: ML → 4 chunks on an ATIS-style wall of text
props.content: **Heuristic verdict:** for a single pilot briefing, you usually want **one** digestible block (or dispatcher-specific rules), not four micro-slices. **Action:** counts this as ML over-split; needs Whisper-shaped training or a max-length policy.

props.title: Regex splits before TAXI on “…GROUND READY FOR TAXI…”
props.content: **Heuristic verdict:** “ready for taxi” behaves like **one** pilot request. **ML kept 1 segment, regex 2** — here ML is **more plausible** for a single-intent pass.

props.title: Regex splits at DIRECT on “…PROCEEDING DIRECT SAN CARLOS”
props.content: **Heuristic verdict:** sounds like **one** continuity (proceeding direct to a fix). **ML kept 1 segment** — regex likely **over-split**.

props.title: “…REPORT BASE” tail
props.content: **Heuristic verdict:** **ambiguous** without the clean-script gold. Could be a second instruction (“report base”) or one compound position report. Flag for phraseology review, not auto-graded.

cd poc/deberta_intent
uv run python tools/spike_boundary_transcripts.py \
  --flights-root ../../data/flights \
  --jsonl ../../data/src/partner_graph/output/fixtures/transcriptions.jsonl \
  --checkpoint checkpoints/boundary-deberta-base \
  --out data/spikes/boundary_transcript_report_full.jsonl

# FlowMind: lev exec --flow .lev/flows/naac-boundary-transcript-spike.flow.yaml

• **No comma ground truth** on the Whisper lines — we’re comparing two heuristics.
• **Filtered ASR rows** (22) were skipped; they might hide tricky duplicates.
• **Promotion** still means wiring `BoundarySplitter` behind config, then grading **downstream readbacks / FMM**, not celebrating segment counts.

**Agents:** you can batch-review JSONL chunks with the same rubric as these four cards — that’s the repeatable human loop until we have labels.
